title: Scoring System for Contact Tracing Prioritisation
text: install package github:

title: Scoring System for Contact Tracing Prioritisation
text: following, read toy dataset included package, contains simulated contact tracing data 30 contacts. contact one exposures, led infection. Data stored inside xlsx file distributed package, practice read data csv xlsx file. row corresponds reported exposure. file contains following information: contact_id: unique identifier contact date: date exposure, indicated integer since first exposure; practice, actual dates format YYYY-MM-DD type: type exposure; , either “household” “funeral” location: location contact; , either “new_city”, “local_town”, “hotspot”; actual names places practice last_visit: date last visit contact, exhibited symptoms; contact visited yet, NA infected: simulation truth, indicating whether contact infected ; known practice, included demonstration purposes onset: simulation truth, indicating day symptom onset infected contacts; may known practice, included demonstration purposes Now imported data R, need convert ctdata object using make_ctdata() (see ?make_ctdata details). Infection probabilities (argument infection_proba) can estimated previous contact tracing data, literature, expert opinion. , assume household exposures 20% probability infection, funeral exposures 90% probability infection. Smaller datasets can visualised using plot():  Next, need specify incubation time distribution, can provided either vector probabilities (giving p(0 day), p(1 day), p(2 days) …) distcrete object returned distcrete::distcrete(). , generate dummy incubation time distribution discretized Gamma:  can now calculate ctscore using ctscore() function, current date day 31 (, real date practice, YYYY-MM-DD format): score indicates, contact, probability visit today lead detecting new case. convenience, can ask ctscore return results formats argument out_type: “ctdata”: returns ctdata object individual data scores (stripping exposures); useful prioritising individuals visits “data.frame”: returns data.frame contact IDs scores “ctdata_full”: ctdata object additional column scores appended exposure database can use simple plot visualise scores contact, can useful prioritising visits:  can look first last scores understand scoring system works. first contact (ID 14) highest score multiple exposures, including several funeral exposures, last seen 16 days ago (day 15, current day 31), ample time symptoms appeared since . second contact (ID 13) high score reasons: multiple exposures, last visit long time ago. last contact (ID 20) low score single ‘weak’ exposure 2 days ago, unlikely individual infected, , unlikely developed symptoms yet. second last contact (ID 29) reported high-risk (funeral) exposure, recent (day 29) unlikely show symptoms just yet. situation third last contact (ID 21), whose funeral exposure yet recent show symptoms just yet, infected. fourth last contact (ID 19) reported multiple weak exposures, last visited yesterday showed symptoms, unlikely infected.
code: library(ctscore) library(rio) library(magrittr) library(tibble) library(dplyr) #>  #> Attaching package: 'dplyr' #> The following objects are masked from 'package:stats': #>  #>     filter, lag #> The following objects are masked from 'package:base': #>  #>     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union library(ggplot2)  ## use the path to your own file in practice  path_to_file <- system.file("toy_ctdata.xlsx", package = "ctscore")  ## read the data in raw_data <- rio::import(path_to_file) %>%    tibble()  raw_data #> # A tibble: 46 × 7 #>    contact_id  date type      location   last_visit infected onset #>         <dbl> <dbl> <chr>     <chr>           <dbl> <lgl>    <dbl> #>  1          1     5 household new_city           13 FALSE       NA #>  2          2    13 funeral   new_city           27 FALSE       NA #>  3          3     6 household local_town         NA FALSE       NA #>  4          4     5 household new_city           NA FALSE       NA #>  5          5    17 funeral   hotspot            22 TRUE        23 #>  6          5    18 household hotspot            22 TRUE        23 #>  7          5    21 household hotspot            22 TRUE        23 #>  8          6    15 funeral   local_town         NA FALSE       NA #>  9          7     9 household local_town         NA FALSE       NA #> 10          8    14 household hotspot            18 TRUE        24 #> # ℹ 36 more rows x <- make_ctdata(   contact_id = raw_data$contact_id,   date = raw_data$date,   type = raw_data$type,   location = raw_data$location,   infection_proba = list(household = 0.2, funeral = 0.9),   last_visit = raw_data$last_visit )  head(x) #>   contact_id date      type   location last_visit p_infection #> 1          1    5 household   new_city         13         0.2 #> 2         10   10 household local_town         NA         0.2 #> 3         11    2 household   new_city         NA         0.2 #> 4         12    7   funeral    hotspot          9         0.9 #> 5         13    9   funeral local_town          9         0.9 #> 6         13   15 household local_town          9         0.2 class(x) #> [1] "ctdata"     "data.frame" plot(x) incub <- distcrete::distcrete(   "gamma",    interval = 1,    shape = 3.5,    scale = 2.5, w = 0 ) plot(   incub$d(0:30), type = "h", lwd = 6, lend = 1,    xlab = "Days since infection",    ylab = "Probability of symptom onset",   main = "Incubation time distribution",   col = 2 ) score <- ctscore(x, incub, current_date = 31) score #>          1         10         11         12         13         14         15  #> 0.19854218 0.19772316 0.20028407 0.89463558 0.90958819 0.97174690 0.19870824  #>         16         17         18         19          2         20         21  #> 0.22232470 0.19754793 0.74628901 0.15502167 0.60000140 0.01314619 0.14895995  #>         22         23         24         25         26         27         28  #> 0.79589928 0.28480854 0.88870951 0.19158821 0.82388155 0.62067571 0.18872827  #>         29          3         30          4          5          6          7  #> 0.05915784 0.19969489 0.89728078 0.19991568 0.79604599 0.84927722 0.19844927  #>          8          9  #> 0.83344774 0.19871356 ## `ctdata` with individual info and  scores, sorted by score res <- ctscore(x, incub, current_date = 31, out_type = "ctdata") %>%      arrange(desc(score)) head(res) #>   contact_id   location last_visit     score #> 1         14    hotspot         15 0.9717469 #> 2         13 local_town          9 0.9095882 #> 3         30 local_town         NA 0.8972808 #> 4         12    hotspot          9 0.8946356 #> 5         24    hotspot         NA 0.8887095 #> 6          6 local_town         NA 0.8492772  ## ctdata object with scores appended, sorted by score res_full <- ctscore(x, incub, current_date = 31, out_type = "ctdata_full") %>%    arrange(desc(score)) ## some wrangling needed to keep the order of contacts in the plot res %>%    mutate(contact_id = factor(contact_id, levels = unique(contact_id))) %>%  ggplot(aes(x = score, y = contact_id)) +    geom_col() +    theme_bw() +    labs(x = "ctscore (probability of detecting symptoms)",         y = "Contact ID",         title = "Contact tracing scoring") head(res_full) #>   contact_id date      type   location last_visit p_infection     score #> 1         14    1 household    hotspot         15         0.2 0.9717469 #> 2         14   11   funeral    hotspot         15         0.9 0.9717469 #> 3         14   13   funeral    hotspot         15         0.9 0.9717469 #> 4         14   20 household    hotspot         15         0.2 0.9717469 #> 5         13    9   funeral local_town          9         0.9 0.9095882 #> 6         13   15 household local_town          9         0.2 0.9095882 tail(res_full) #>    contact_id date      type   location last_visit p_infection      score #> 41         19    6 household    hotspot         30         0.2 0.15502167 #> 42         19   12 household    hotspot         30         0.2 0.15502167 #> 43         19   13 household    hotspot         30         0.2 0.15502167 #> 44         21   27   funeral    hotspot         29         0.9 0.14895995 #> 45         29   29   funeral local_town         NA         0.9 0.05915784 #> 46         20   29 household    hotspot         NA         0.2 0.01314619

what: Add/change infection probabilities in a ctdata object — add_infection_proba
title: Add/change infection probabilities in a ctdata object — add_infection_proba
text: function can used add change infection probabilities ctdata object. useful changing probabilities infection different types exposures. Probabilities provided named list must one probability exposure type ctdata object.

title: Add/change infection probabilities in a ctdata object — add_infection_proba
Add/change infection probabilities in a ctdata object — add_infection_proba
text: x ctdata object proba named list probabilities exposure type

title: Add/change infection probabilities in a ctdata object — add_infection_proba
text: ctdata object updated infection probabilities

title: Add/change infection probabilities in a ctdata object — add_infection_proba
text: Thibaut Jombart

title: Calculate contact tracing score — ctscore
text: function calculates probability detecting symptoms contact given day given exposure follow-history, described Jombart et al. 2026.

title: Calculate contact tracing score — ctscore
code: ctscore(   x,   incub,   current_date = Sys.Date(),   out_type = c("vector", "data.frame", "ctdata", "ctdata_full") )

title: Calculate contact tracing score — ctscore
text: x ctdata object returned make_ctdata() incub incubation period distribution; can provided vector probabilities giving p(0 day), p(1 day), p(2 days) ... distcrete object returned distcrete::distcrete() current_date current date, provided either numeric value Date; defaults current date returned Sys.Date() out_type character indicating type output return; can either "vector" (default) return named vector scores, "data.frame" return data frame contact IDs scores, "ctdata" return ctdata object individual data scores, "ctdata_full" append scores original ctdata exposure data

title: Calculate contact tracing score — ctscore
text: named numeric vector giving probability detecting symptoms contact, names corresponding contact IDs.

title: Calculate contact tracing score — ctscore
text: Thibaut Jombart

title: Calculate contact tracing score — ctscore
code: ## make dummy contact tracing data x <- make_ctdata(   contact_id = c(1, 1, 2, 3, 4),    date = Sys.Date() - c(6, 4, 5, 1, 5),   type = c("normal", "funeral", "normal", "normal", "null"),   location = "some-town",   infection_proba = list(normal = 0.2, funeral = 0.9, null = 0),   last_visit = Sys.Date() - c(4, 2, 1, 1, 3) )  ## make a dummy incubation time distribution, specifying the PMF from 0 to  ## 7 days here incub <- c(0, 0, 1, 2, 4, 3, 2, 1)  ## get results res <- ctscore(x, incub) res #>         1         2         3         4  #> 0.5266667 0.1000000 0.0000000 0.0000000   ## other useful shape for results: a ctdata object of individuals data with  ## scores appended res <- ctscore(x, incub, out_type = "ctdata") res #>   contact_id  location last_visit     score #> 1          1 some-town 2026-07-03 0.5266667 #> 3          2 some-town 2026-07-06 0.1000000 #> 4          3 some-town 2026-07-06 0.0000000 #> 5          4 some-town 2026-07-04 0.0000000  ## other example using `distcrete` to build the incubation time distribution incub <- distcrete::distcrete("gamma", interval = 1, shape = 2, scale = 2.5, w = 0) res <- ctscore(x, incub) res #>          1          2          3          4  #> 0.36501065 0.04806079 0.02763199 0.00000000   ## trying other output shapes ### data.frame with individual data res_df <- ctscore(x, incub, out_type = "data.frame") res_df #>   contact_id      score #> 1          1 0.36501065 #> 2          2 0.04806079 #> 3          3 0.02763199 #> 4          4 0.00000000  ### ctdata object of individuals with scores appended res_ctdata <- ctscore(x, incub, out_type = "ctdata") res_ctdata #>   contact_id  location last_visit      score #> 1          1 some-town 2026-07-03 0.36501065 #> 3          2 some-town 2026-07-06 0.04806079 #> 4          3 some-town 2026-07-06 0.02763199 #> 5          4 some-town 2026-07-04 0.00000000   ### same, with all original exposure data res_ctdata_full <- ctscore(x, incub, out_type = "ctdata_full") res_ctdata_full #>   contact_id       date    type  location last_visit p_infection      score #> 1          1 2026-07-01  normal some-town 2026-07-03         0.2 0.36501065 #> 2          1 2026-07-03 funeral some-town 2026-07-05         0.9 0.36501065 #> 3          2 2026-07-02  normal some-town 2026-07-06         0.2 0.04806079 #> 4          3 2026-07-06  normal some-town 2026-07-06         0.2 0.02763199 #> 5          4 2026-07-02    null some-town 2026-07-04         0.0 0.00000000

what: Extract individual data from a ctdata object — extract_indiv_data
title: Extract individual data from a ctdata object — extract_indiv_data
text: ctdata objects used store exposure data; contacts may report multiple exposures, corresponding individual data (e.g. location, data last visit) repeated. function extracts individual data output single row individual, effectively stripping exposure data.

title: Extract individual data from a ctdata object — extract_indiv_data
Extract individual data from a ctdata object — extract_indiv_data
text: x ctdata object returned make_ctdata() sim_ctdata()

title: Extract individual data from a ctdata object — extract_indiv_data
text: Thibaut Jombart

title: Extract individual data from a ctdata object — extract_indiv_data
code: ## with a simple ctdata x <- make_ctdata(   contact_id = c(1, 1, 2, 3, 3),    date = Sys.Date() - c(6, 4, 2, 2, 3),   type = c("normal", "funeral", "normal", "normal", "funeral"),   location = c("some-town", "some-town", "some-town", "sincity", "sincity"),   infection_proba = list(normal = 0.2, funeral = 0.9),   last_visit = Sys.Date() - c(4, 4, 1, NA, NA) ) x #>   contact_id       date    type  location last_visit p_infection #> 1          1 2026-07-01  normal some-town 2026-07-03         0.2 #> 2          1 2026-07-03 funeral some-town 2026-07-03         0.9 #> 3          2 2026-07-05  normal some-town 2026-07-06         0.2 #> 4          3 2026-07-04 funeral   sincity       <NA>         0.9 #> 5          3 2026-07-05  normal   sincity       <NA>         0.2  extract_indiv_data(x) #>   contact_id  location last_visit #> 1          1 some-town 2026-07-03 #> 3          2 some-town 2026-07-06 #> 4          3   sincity       <NA>  ## with a ctdata object containing scores (returned by ctscore()) scores <- ctscore(x,    incub = c(0, 0, 1, 2, 4, 3, 2, 1),    current_date = Sys.Date(),    out_type = "ctdata" )  scores #>   contact_id  location last_visit      score #> 1          1 some-town 2026-07-03 0.57102564 #> 3          2 some-town 2026-07-06 0.01538462 #> 4          3   sincity       <NA> 0.20923077 extract_indiv_data(scores) #>   contact_id  location last_visit      score #> 1          1 some-town 2026-07-03 0.57102564 #> 3          2 some-town 2026-07-06 0.01538462 #> 4          3   sincity       <NA> 0.20923077

title: Build a ctdata object — make_ctdata
text: constructor build ctdata object different inputs describing past exposures follow-history different individuals.

title: Build a ctdata object — make_ctdata
code: make_ctdata(   contact_id,   date,   type = "default",   location = "default",   infection_proba = list(default = 0),   last_visit )

title: Build a ctdata object — make_ctdata
text: contact_id character numeric vector indicating identifiers contacts; converted character already date Date, numeric, character vector indicating dates exposures; character converted Date using .Date, expected formats "%Y-%m-%d" "%Y/%m/%d"; fancier conversions done creating ctdata object type character used describe type exposure; defaults default location character used describe geographic location contact; defaults default infection_proba list named numeric values, indicating probability infection given contact; defaults list 'default' exposure probability infection 0 last_visit date last visit contact, exhibited symptoms; type provided must match date; contact visited yet, NA

title: Build a ctdata object — make_ctdata
text: ctdata object, validated ordered (contact ID date exposure) data.frame designed used ctscore function.

title: Build a ctdata object — make_ctdata
text: Thibaut Jombart

title: Build a ctdata object — make_ctdata
code: x <- make_ctdata(   contact_id = c(1, 1, 2, 3),    date = Sys.Date() - c(6, 4, 2, 2),   type = c("normal", "funeral", "normal", "normal"),   location = "some-town",   infection_proba = list(normal = 0.2, funeral = 0.9),   last_visit = Sys.Date() - c(4, 4, 1, NA) ) x #>   contact_id       date    type  location last_visit p_infection #> 1          1 2026-07-01  normal some-town 2026-07-03         0.2 #> 2          1 2026-07-03 funeral some-town 2026-07-03         0.9 #> 3          2 2026-07-05  normal some-town 2026-07-06         0.2 #> 4          3 2026-07-05  normal some-town       <NA>         0.2 class(x) #> [1] "ctdata"     "data.frame"

title: Plot a contact-tracing timeline — plot.ctdata
text: Produces timeline plot contact-tracing data, one row per contact along time axis. Contacts ordered first exposure day.

title: Plot a contact-tracing timeline — plot.ctdata
text: x ctdata object returned make_ctdata(), sim_ctdata object returned sim_ctdata(). ... currently ignored.

title: Plot a contact-tracing timeline — plot.ctdata
text: ggplot object.

title: Plot a contact-tracing timeline — plot.ctdata
text: Cyril Geismar

title: Plot a contact-tracing timeline — plot.ctdata
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ ## simulated data (also shows symptom onset) plot(sim_ctdata())  } # }

title: Simulate contact tracing data — sim_ctdata
text: Simulate contact tracing data set contacts. contact one exposures, probability causing infection. Infected contacts develop symptoms incubation period.

title: Simulate contact tracing data — sim_ctdata
code: sim_ctdata(   n_contacts = 100,   duration = 30,   incub = 1:7,   n_exposures = list(default = 1),   infection_proba = list(default = 0.1),   locations = list(default = 1) )

title: Simulate contact tracing data — sim_ctdata
text: n_contacts Number contacts simulate. duration Length exposure period (days). incub Integer vector incubation periods (days), sampled replacement. n_exposures Named list giving number exposures per contact location. infection_proba Named list giving probability infection exposure type. locations Named list giving probability location sampled contact.

title: Simulate contact tracing data — sim_ctdata
text: object class c("sim_ctdata", "ctdata", "data.frame") one row per exposure. carries standard ctdata columns (contact_id, date, type, location, last_visit, p_infection) plus simulation truth infected (logical, per contact) onset (symptom onset day, NA infected).

title: Simulate contact tracing data — sim_ctdata
text: Cyril Geismar

what: Simulate follow-up for contact tracing data — sim_followup
title: Simulate follow-up for contact tracing data — sim_followup
text: function adds simulated follow-ctdata object generated make_ctdata() simulated via sim_ctdata(). function simulates follow-individual ctdata object, based specified follow-time probability followed . function returns modified ctdata object simulated follow-data added.

title: Simulate follow-up for contact tracing data — sim_followup
code: sim_followup(   x,   time = 1,   delay = 1,   duration = 21,   coverage = 0,   strategy = c("random", "geo_random", "ctscore", "geo_ctscore") )

title: Simulate follow-up for contact tracing data — sim_followup
text: x ctdata object time integer indicating number days run simulation ; starts recent date x, follow-history exposures; defaults 1 day delay integer minimum delay follow-start, first exposure concerned contact; defaults 1 - visit can start day first exposure duration integer indicating number days last exposure contact followed ; usually determined according incubation time distribution; defaults 21 days coverage proportion contacts visited time step; defaults 0 - follow-strategy character indicating follow-strategy use simulations; currently available values : "random"; see details section information

title: Simulate follow-up for contact tracing data — sim_followup
text: Available follow-strategies (strategy argument) include: "random": individuals visited random every day simulation "geo_random": locations prioritized random every day simulation; many contacts possible visited first chosen location, capacity remains, second, third, etc. "ctscore": individuals prioritized highest ctscore every day simulation "geo_ctscore": geographic locations prioritized according highest ctscore every day simulation; many contacts possible visited first chosen location, capacity remains, second, third, etc.

title: Simulate follow-up for contact tracing data — sim_followup
text: Thibaut Jombart
